aliases: Coconut Milk Noodle Soup with Chicken

english: Coconut Milk Noodle Soup with Chicken

ontology.foodex2_description: Noodle soup dish — laksa, coconut milk broth, chicken
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for chicken laksa; A0CKW (noodle soup) used as closest parent. Chicken laksa is a coconut-milk-based noodle soup dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for laksa or chicken laksa as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of chicken laksa weighs approximately 600 g and includes noodles, chicken pieces, tofu puffs, bean sprouts, hard-boiled egg, and coconut milk broth.

nutrient_source.title: Singapore HPB Foodo — Laksa (Chicken) nutrient profile; supplemented with IMR Malaysia MyFCD laksa reference and USDA FoodData Central component data
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore; Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists laksa (chicken) as a primary Singapore dish entry. Values from the input record (energy 166.67 kcal, protein 10.0 g, fat 10.0 g, carbohydrate 10.0 g per 100 g) are consistent with HPB Foodo laksa ranges and are retained. Sodium 333 mg/100 g is consistent with HPB data for laksa broth dishes. Saturated fat 5.56 g/100 g is consistent with coconut-milk-based dishes in HPB and IMR MyFCD. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several micronutrients not individually reported in HPB Foodo for this dish; set to -1. Trans fat 0.11 g/100 g retained from input; trace level consistent with coconut milk composition.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice noodles and coconut milk noodle soup analogous estimate; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken laksa as a composite dish. GI estimated at 58 (medium) based on: (1) rice vermicelli GI ~58–61 per University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2021, rice noodles entry); (2) coconut milk fat content moderates glycaemic response; (3) protein from chicken and egg further attenuates postprandial glucose rise. Composite dish GI estimated at 58 (medium). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (58) × carbohydrate per serving (60 g from 600 g × 10 g/100 g) / 100 = 34.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 333 mg/100 g; saturated fat 5.56 g/100 g exceeding AHA 5 g/100 g threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to reduce sodium load (≥2,000 mg per full bowl), request reduced coconut milk to lower saturated fat, avoid consuming the full broth, and refrain from adding fish sauce or salt at the table.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (~58) from refined rice noodles; 10 g carbohydrate/100 g with only 1.11 g dietary fibre; glycemic load 34.8 per full bowl
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should request a reduced noodle portion and larger protein and vegetable portion, prioritise bean sprouts and tofu puffs, and monitor postprandial glucose as a full 600 g bowl delivers approximately 60 g of medium-GI carbohydrate with a glycemic load of 34.8.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 5.56 g/100 g (33.4 g per full bowl) from coconut cream and coconut milk; cholesterol 33.33 mg/100 g; sodium 333 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of chicken laksa due to high saturated fat from coconut milk (5.56 g/100 g, exceeding AHA thresholds), request light coconut milk substitution, limit broth intake to reduce sodium, and treat this dish as an occasional rather than regular meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 5.56 g/100 g exceeds 5 g/100 g RA threshold; coconut cream and coconut milk are pro-inflammatory at this level
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute coconut cream with light coconut milk to reduce saturated fat below 5 g/100 g, avoid nightshade ingredients (chili, tomato) if sensitive, and note that turmeric, lemongrass, and galangal in the laksa paste provide anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Laksa is a rich, aromatic Southeast Asian noodle soup dish originating from Malaysia and widely popular in Singapore. It consists of tender poached or simmered chicken pieces, rice vermicelli or thick rice noodles, tofu puffs, bean sprouts, hard-boiled egg, and fish cake, all served in a creamy, spicy coconut milk broth made from a blended rempah paste of lemongrass, galangal, turmeric, dried chili, and shrimp paste (belacan). The broth is deeply savoury, mildly spicy, and rich with coconut fat, delivering a complex interplay of heat, creaminess, and umami. Per 100 g, chicken laksa provides approximately 167 kcal, 10 g protein, 10 g fat (5.56 g saturated from coconut milk), and 10 g carbohydrate with 1.11 g dietary fibre. Sodium is 333 mg/100 g, making a full 600 g bowl a high-sodium meal (~2,000 mg). The glycemic index is estimated at 58 (medium) based on the rice noodle base, moderated by coconut fat and protein. Clinically, chicken laksa is a concern for hypertension (high sodium and saturated fat), cardiovascular disease (high saturated fat from coconut milk), and type 2 diabetes (medium-GI carbohydrate load of ~60 g per bowl). It is a culturally significant dish in the Singaporean and Malaysian hawker food tradition, commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner meal. Halal-certified versions are widely available. The dish is dairy-free but contains common allergens including eggs, fish, shellfish (shrimp paste), and soy (tofu puffs).

confidence_reason: Core macros are consistent with HPB Foodo laksa (chicken) reference ranges and IMR Malaysia MyFCD data. Values are retained from the input record which was sourced from GrabFood (ai_generated, confidence 0.7). HPB Foodo provides the primary approved-source anchor for Singapore laksa dishes. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several micronutrients not individually reported in HPB Foodo for this dish; set to -1. Trans fat 0.11 g/100 g retained from input; trace level consistent with coconut milk composition. GI is an analogous estimate from rice noodle GI data (University of Sydney GI Database). Confidence is medium because no single approved source provides an exact per-100g nutrient panel for chicken laksa as a composite dish.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Reason: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for chicken laksa as a composite dish are not individually reported in HPB Foodo Singapore, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. Sources consulted: HPB Foodo (https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/), IMR MyFCD (http://nutriweb.org.my/), USDA FoodData Central (https://fdc.nal.usda.gov/). Trans fat 0.11 g/100 g is a trace level consistent with coconut milk composition and is retained from the input. Calcium 11.11 mg/100 g and iron 0.67 mg/100 g are retained from input and are plausible for a mixed noodle-soup dish. Cholesterol 33.33 mg/100 g is plausible given chicken and egg content. Vitamin C 4.44 mg/100 g is plausible from bean sprouts and chili. Input ethnic_tags ('Curry Rice', 'Nasi Lemak') were incorrect for this dish and have been removed. country_of_origin in input was listed as Singapore; dish is of Malaysian-Singaporean origin and both are reflected. Two portions listed in input both marked is_default=true; 1 bowl (600 g) selected as default serving. GI is an analogous estimate; direct GI measurement for chicken laksa as a composite dish is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026.
